coredata.dc:title: Chitosan/gelatin-based multifunctional films integrated with sulfur-functionalized chitin for active packaging applications
coredata.dc:creator: Khan, Ajahar
Riahi, Zohreh
Kim, Jun Tae
Rhim, Jong-Whan
coredata.dc:description: Sulfur-functionalized chitin composite (S-chitin) was prepared through sulfur-amine complex formation using ethylenediamine. The S-chitin exhibited excellent antioxidant, antimicrobial, and UV-barrier properties. Chitosan/gelatin (CS/Gel)-based active packaging films containing S-chitin (CS/Gel@S-chitin) were prepared and characterized. The addition of S-chitin enhanced the tensile strength and elongation at the break of the CS/Gel film by 18.4% and 42.2%, respectively, and slightly lowered the transparency of the film but enhanced the UV-blocking with 98.7% UV-A and 100% UV-B light screening. The composite films showed strong antioxidant efficacy with 100% ABTS and 95.9% DPPH radical scavenging potential. The films exhibited effective antimicrobial activity and completely stopped the growth of L. monocytogenes and E. coli after 3 h and 12 h of incubation, respectively. Packaging chicken and storing at 4 °C for 20 days showed that the chicken packaged with the CS/Gel@S-chitin films preserved the quality of the chicken of appearance, pH, TBARS (thiobarbituric acid reactive substance), and TVC (total viable colony count). The multifunctional S-chitin incorporated CS/Gel films have a high potential for active packaging applications.

Antioxidant and antimicrobial activities of the films
Application of CS/Gel@S-chitin films for chicken preservation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: A binary polymer composite solution was prepared separately by dissolving CS and Gel. The CS solution was made by dissolving 2% (w/v) of polymer powder in 100 mL of aqueous acetic acid 1% (v/v) followed by adding 30% (w/w based on chitosan) glycerol as a plasticizer under stirring at 65 ± 2 °C for 5 h. Then filtered the CS solution using cheesecloth to remove impurities and undissolved particles. The Gel solution was obtained by dissolving 2% (w/v) and 30% (w/w of Gel) glycerol in distilled water (100 mL) with vigorous stirring for 1 h at 75 ± 2 °C. The binary solution mixtures in a 1:1 ratio were homogenously mixed with stirring at 50 °C for 1 h, then different contents of S-chitin (2% and 4%) were incorporated. The mixed solution was stirred for 1.5 h at 50 °C for an even distribution of added fillers to the polymer matrix. Finally, the solution mixture (200 mL) was cast over the glass plate coated with Teflon film (30 cm × 24 cm) for 48 h at 25 °C (Fig. 1). The dried films were prudently peeled and kept in a humidity chamber (model FX 1077, Jeio Tech Co. Ltd., Ansan, Korea) controlled at 25 °C with 50% RH for 48 h before further investigations. In addition, the neat CS/Gel films (without S-chitin) were also developed following the same approach (Table S1, Supporting Information). The produced films were designated CS/Gel, CS/Gel@S-chitin2, and CS/Gel@S-chitin4, depending on the content of S-chitin.The characterization methods, including surface morphology, functional groups, optical properties, surface color, chemical composition, mechanical properties, water contact angle (WCA), and water vapor permeability (WVP), for the fabricated S-chitin and CS/Gel@S-chitin films are provided in the Supporting Information.The antibacterial activity of S-chitin was evaluated against Gram-positive (S. aureus and L. monocytogeneses) and Gram-negative (E. coli and S. enterica) using the agar well diffusion assay, and their MIC/MBC values are listed in Table S3 (Supporting Information). The agar well diffusion analysis shows that the neat chitin did not show growth inhibition for any of the four bacterial strains (Fig. 3e). However, the S-chitin unveiled a distinctive antibacterial effect against S. aureus and L. monocytogenes (Gram-positive) bacterial strains, but less antibacterial effect against E. coli and S. enterica (Gram-negative). In addition, the S-chitin showed growth inhibition zones of ∼6.6 mm, ∼4.7 mm, and ∼2.1 mm, ∼1.5 mm inhibition zone against L. monocytogenes and S. aureus and then S. enterica and E. coli, respectively. However, no inhibition zone was observed for the neat chitin against all four bacterial strains (Table S3). Likewise, the MIC/MBC evaluation depicted that the antibacterial efficacy of S-chitin was similar to that of the agar well diffusion assay. The MIC/MBC of S-chitin against L. monocytogenes and S. aureus were 200/300 and 300/400 μg/mL, while S. enterica and E. coli were 600/700 and 800/900 μg/mL, respectively. On the other side, the neat chitin had MIC/MBC >1000 μg/mL against all four bacterial strains, suggesting a negligible bactericidal effect (Table S3). Therefore, it can be validated that the antibacterial effect of S-chitin is ascribed to the functionalization of chitin by the sulfur. These antibacterial properties of S-chitin are reliable with the results of Dop et al., who recently investigated that a high-sulfur polymer developed using reverse vulcanization showed strong antibacterial activity against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria (Dop et al., 2023), and they suggested that the interaction of polymer particles with cellular thiols could be a possible mechanism of action against bacterial cells. Sulfur in various forms (e.g., sulfur nanoparticles, sulfur polymers, or sulfur quantum dots) has been reported to exhibit more substantial bactericidal impacts against Gram-positive bacterial strains than Gram-negative bacterial strains (Dop et al., 2023; Priyadarshi et al., 2023a; Riahi et al., 2022; Saedi et al., 2020). Several possible mechanisms have been projected regarding the antimicrobial efficacy of different forms of sulfur particles. For instance, interactions with biological molecules on the surface of bacteria can cause cell lysis due to membrane rupturing and produce toxic H2S gas when lipids and proteins interact with their thiol groups and interact with DNA to cause cell death by denaturing DNA (Dop et al., 2023; Priyadarshi et al., 2023a; Riahi et al., 2022; Saedi et al., 2020). Also, the micro-sized elemental sulfur's antibacterial action was caused by its binding to an enzyme with a –SH functional group (Saedi et al., 2020). However, the antimicrobial results depicted that the antibacterial function of S-chitin was more potent in Gram-positive bacteria (single thick peptidoglycan layer) than in Gram-negative bacteria (thin peptidoglycan layer with an outer cell wall), which can correspond to the variation in their cell wall structure (Khan et al., 2023b).This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (2022R1A2B5B02001422) and the Brain Pool Program funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning through the National Research Foundation of Korea (2022H1D3A2A02092788).

This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea ( NRF ) grant funded by the Korean government ( MSIT ) (2022R1A2B5B02001422) and the Brain Pool Program funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning through the National Research Foundation of Korea (2022H1D3A2A02092788).

head.ce:title: Chitosan/gelatin-based multifunctional films integrated with sulfur-functionalized chitin for active packaging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul, 02447, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul, 02447, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sulfur-functionalized chitin composite (S-chitin) was prepared through sulfur-amine complex formation using ethylenediamine. The S-chitin exhibited excellent antioxidant, antimicrobial, and UV-barrier properties. Chitosan/gelatin (CS/Gel)-based active packaging films containing S-chitin (CS/Gel@S-chitin) were prepared and characterized. The addition of S-chitin enhanced the tensile strength and elongation at the break of the CS/Gel film by 18.4% and 42.2%, respectively, and slightly lowered the transparency of the film but enhanced the UV-blocking with 98.7% UV-A and 100% UV-B light screening. The composite films showed strong antioxidant efficacy with 100% ABTS and 95.9% DPPH radical scavenging potential. The films exhibited effective antimicrobial activity and completely stopped the growth of L. monocytogenes and E. coli after 3 h and 12 h of incubation, respectively. Packaging chicken and storing at 4 °C for 20 days showed that the chicken packaged with the CS/Gel@S-chitin films preserved the quality of the chicken of appearance, pH, TBARS (thiobarbituric acid reactive substance), and TVC (total viable colony count). The multifunctional S-chitin incorporated CS/Gel films have a high potential for active packaging applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Meat products are prone to bacterial contamination during processing and cannot be stored even at low temperatures due to high constant biological processes (oxidation) and moisture content (Kong et al., 2023; Luo et al., 2023). Food waste and its decomposition are, therefore, a major concern worldwide (Khan et al., 2023a). Further, the excessive use of petroleum-based plastics in all industries, including packaging, seriously threatens living organisms' ecological sustainability worldwide. Therefore, there is a need to design innovative, sustainable, simple and safe active packaging materials to extend the shelf life and maintain the quality of processed meat products (Ezati et al., 2023; Siddiqui et al., 2023; Tammina et al., 2023). In this context, biodegradable polymers appear to be a promising alternative for producing sustainable packaging materials, as they can be produced from renewable biomass such as feedstocks and agricultural waste (Riahi et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2023). Preservation of meat products using biomass-derived materials appears to be an effective and safe method that has recently received much attention (Khan et al., 2023b, 2024). In addition, it reduces concerns about the depletion of natural resources and environmental problems while adding high-added value to residual biomass.

One promising biodegradable packaging film material is gelatin (Gel), a promising option for sustainable active packaging due to its wide availability, cost-effectiveness, and excellent film-forming ability (Khan et al., 2023c). Gelatin is a protein obtained by hydrolyzing collagen and is widely employed as a film-forming ingredient in health supplements and pharmaceuticals (Khan et al., 2023c; Khan et al., 2023b; Yan et al., 2023). However, the hydrophilic nature of Gel limits its utilization in food packaging, which can be overcome by mixing chitosan (CS) with the Gel to create a composite film. After cellulose, CS is the next most abundant biopolymer in nature and is highly regarded by industry and academia for food applications due to its biocompatibility, brilliant film-forming capacity, non-toxicity, excellent printability, transparency, processability, excellent mechanical properties, and economic feasibility (Liao et al., 2023; Priyadarshi & Rhim, 2020). Combining CS and Gel (CS/Gel) can produce an integrated composite network because of the intermolecular hydrogen interactions among polymer chains, improving composite films' mechanical and physicochemical characteristics (Khan et al., 2023c).

CS/Gel film has many advantages but is expected to be more useful for active food packaging applications if it has an appropriate barrier and antioxidant and antibacterial abilities. Therefore, major advances are needed to ensure widespread use, especially concerning functionalities and barrier properties. Several proposed methods focus on using a combination of materials to obtain functional films with desired properties (Khan et al., 2023d; Lin, Mei, et al., 2023). For example, the use of chitin nanocrystals (a bio-based nanomaterial) has been exposed to perform as a nucleating agent for poly(lactic acid) (PLA), and the homogeneous dispersion of these nanoparticles in the polymer blend enhanced physicochemical properties of PLA film for packaging applications (Singh et al., 2020). In contrast, organic nanoparticles or composites are frequently produced from food processing by-products and offer several advantages over inorganic nanoparticles, such as being non-toxic, edible, non-dose dependent, and compatible with biodegradable polymers (Sahraee et al., 2017). According to a literature review, several investigations have found the effects of N-chitin (nano-chitin) on the barrier, thermal and mechanical characteristics of biopolymers. For example, Wang et al. reported that incorporating N-chitin improved the physicochemical features of poly(3-hydroxybutyrate-co-3-hydroxyvalerate) composite films (Wang et al., 2012). Encapsulation of chitin nanoparticles as functional fillers in soy protein (Lu et al., 2004), chitosan (Rubentheren et al., 2015), carrageenan (Shankar et al., 2015), and starch (Chang et al., 2010) matrices have also been analyzed to enhance the film's properties for food packaging purposes.

This study used elemental sulfur-functionalized chitin composites to fabricate CS/Gel-based multifunctional films for active food packaging applications. To this end, a sulfur-amine chemistry-based strategy was adopted to prepare S-chitin complexes as functional materials to improve packaging films' physicochemical and bioactive properties. Sulfur is one of the most common and biologically active elements on Earth, an odorless and non-toxic element, and its most common allotrope occurs most often in nature in the form of the element in the cyclic S8 structure (Priyadarshi et al., 2023a). Elemental sulfur (S8) is a vital intermediate in the sulfur biogeochemical cycle of ecosystems and imparts a crucial role in the biochemical cycles of all living organisms (Priyadarshi et al., 2021, Priyadarshi et al., 2023a). Elemental sulfur and its compounds have applications in pharmaceuticals, biomedicine, chemicals, and fertilizers. Numerous studies have been conducted on sulfur nanomaterials for a variety of purposes, including food packaging, environmental sensing, catalysis, antimicrobials, energy storage, and electronics (Priyadarshi et al., 2023a).

On the other hand, chitin has been the subject of numerous studies, including drug delivery, biosensors, composites, membranes, fibers, cosmetics, food packaging, and food additives, due to its desirable properties in terms of biodegradability, biocompatibility, multifunctionality, and non-toxicity (Hai et al., 2020). Therefore, functionalization of chitin with elemental sulfur was attempted using a solution deposition approach that is easily scalable, cost-effective, time-saving, and requires low energy consumption (Wang et al., 2014). On the other hand, sulfur-amine complexes have previously been employed as sulfur sources to develop metal sulfide nanocrystals (Wang et al., 2014). It has been reported that these sulfur-amine composites can be used to quantitatively deposit sulfur on chitin without needing pre- and post-treatment during heat treatment (Wang et al., 2014).

Therefore, this study manufactured and tested a functional food packaging film by adding a functional S-chitin complex obtained by decorating elemental sulfur, a waste product of the petroleum refining process, to the CS/Gel matrix (CS/Gel@S-chitin) and tested their effects for quality enhancement and the preservation of chicken. The S-chitin was characterized to ensure that sulfur was uniformly encapsulated with the chitin matrix. Also, the physicochemical properties of the CS/Gel@S-chitin films were thoroughly investigated. Further, the impact of S-chitin incorporation on UV blocking, mechanical, surface wettability, water vapor barrier, and antibacterial and antioxidant characteristics was assessed.

Gelatin (Gel) (Fish gelatin, Food-grade, Type A, 200 Bloom) was supplied by Gel-Tec Co., Ltd. (Seoul, Korea), whereas chitosan (CS) (MW: 190,000–310,000 based on viscosity, degree of deacetylation: 75–85%, viscosity: 200–800 cP at 1% acetic acid), and chitin (from shrimp shell, practical grade) were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA), respectively. Ethylene diamine anhydrous (EDA, analytical grade), sublimated sulfur powder (>99%) procured from Daejung Chemicals & Metals Co., Ltd. (Siheung, Gyeonggi-do, Korea). BHI (brain heart infusion broth) and TSB (tryptic soy broth) (Duksan Pure Chemicals Co., Ltd., Ansan, Gyeonggi-do, South Korea) were used for bacterial culture. For packaging purposes, the chicken meat was obtained from a local supermarket (Seoul, Korea).

Functionalization of chitin was carried out by following the sulfur-amine chemistry-based approach of Wang et al. (2014) with slight modification. For this, 4 g of sublimed sulfur was dissolved in 20 mL EDA under mild mechanical agitation at 25 °C (room temperature). After adding sulfur to the EDA solution, the rapid color change of the solution mixture from yellow to dark brown demonstrates the dissolution of sulfur in the sulfur-EDA solution (Fig. 1 ). Separately, 2.5 g of chitin powder was dispersed in 250 mL of 0.1 M HCl solution using sonication (750 W, Sonics & Materials, Inc. USA) for 15 min. Afterward, the sulfur-EDA solution was added dropwise to the chitin dispersion at a rate of ∼2.5 mL min−1 with constant stirring at 50 °C. The reaction was permitted to proceed further with stirring for 16 h under airtight conditions at 50 °C. After cooling to room temperature, unreacted components were filtered and rinsed thoroughly several times with distilled water via centrifugation at 10,000 rpm (12298×g) (Labogene 1248, Gyrozen Co., Ltd., Daejeon, South Korea). Finally, the S-chitin complex was obtained after drying at 50 °C overnight.

A binary polymer composite solution was prepared separately by dissolving CS and Gel. The CS solution was made by dissolving 2% (w/v) of polymer powder in 100 mL of aqueous acetic acid 1% (v/v) followed by adding 30% (w/w based on chitosan) glycerol as a plasticizer under stirring at 65 ± 2 °C for 5 h. Then filtered the CS solution using cheesecloth to remove impurities and undissolved particles. The Gel solution was obtained by dissolving 2% (w/v) and 30% (w/w of Gel) glycerol in distilled water (100 mL) with vigorous stirring for 1 h at 75 ± 2 °C. The binary solution mixtures in a 1:1 ratio were homogenously mixed with stirring at 50 °C for 1 h, then different contents of S-chitin (2% and 4%) were incorporated. The mixed solution was stirred for 1.5 h at 50 °C for an even distribution of added fillers to the polymer matrix. Finally, the solution mixture (200 mL) was cast over the glass plate coated with Teflon film (30 cm × 24 cm) for 48 h at 25 °C (Fig. 1). The dried films were prudently peeled and kept in a humidity chamber (model FX 1077, Jeio Tech Co. Ltd., Ansan, Korea) controlled at 25 °C with 50% RH for 48 h before further investigations. In addition, the neat CS/Gel films (without S-chitin) were also developed following the same approach (Table S1, Supporting Information). The produced films were designated CS/Gel, CS/Gel@S-chitin2, and CS/Gel@S-chitin4, depending on the content of S-chitin.

The characterization methods, including surface morphology, functional groups, optical properties, surface color, chemical composition, mechanical properties, water contact angle (WCA), and water vapor permeability (WVP), for the fabricated S-chitin and CS/Gel@S-chitin films are provided in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Packaging application of films in chicken preservation
ce:section.ce:para: For the packaging test, 20 g of fresh chicken packed in the CS/Gel@S-chitin films (8 cm × 8 cm) were sealed with a polyethylene wrap and kept in the low-temperature incubator at 4 °C throughout the test period (20 days). Chicken packaged with the neat CS-Gel was used for comparison with the CS/Gel@S-chitin2 and CS/Gel@S-chitin4 films. Chicken test samples were examined for color, quality, pH, TBARS (thiobarbituric acid reactive substances), and bacterial contamination during packaging. The appearance quality of the test samples was evaluated by determining the pH and chroma color signals. The TVC (total viable colony count) method was used to examine the growth of bacteria in packaged samples from each group. The 0.1% test sample was homogenized in peptone water (45 mL) for 180 s through a stomacher (BagMixer® 400, Interscience, France). Homogenous aliquots (after serial dilutions) of 1 mL were then spread onto 3 M™ Petrifilm counting plates. Data are listed as log CFU/g based on colony count after incubation for 48 h at 37 °C. Test sample quality assessment was performed every 5 days throughout storage, and quality measurements were repeated thrice.

The film properties were measured in triplicate with individually prepared films as replicated experimental units. One-way variance analysis (ANOVA) was performed, and the significance of each mean property value was determined (p < 0.05) by Duncan's multiple range test using the statistical analysis (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA) software.

When elemental sulfur (S8) is dissolved in EDA, ring-opening polymerization occurs, and sulfur is converted to reactive sulfur radicals and polysulfide ions (Wang et al., 2014). Here, the octatomic ring of S8 is open with the direct nucleophilic attack of nitrogen, which produces open-chain alkylammonium polysulfides (Nakamura & Y, 1971; Thomson et al., 2011). When sulfur-EDA is added dropwise to diluted HCl (chitin dispersion), the precursor solution decomposes, and the newly recovered sulfur molecules precipitate on the chitin surface to form S-chitin complexes (Wang et al., 2014). However, due to the hydrophobicity of S8 and chitin, they cannot form stable dispersions in water (Fig. 2 a). Nevertheless, the deposition of EDA-imposed sulfur around the chitin core provides the S-chitin complex with excellent physicochemical and functional properties and forms a stable dispersion in aqueous media (Fig. 2a). After functionalization, the electron-rich –NH2 group of chitin excites the surface –OH group of the S-chitin complex and helps to interact with water molecules and promote stable dispersion in aqueous medium. Further integration with CS/Gel matrices improves the film light barrier and bioactive performances desired for active food packaging applications.

The FTIR spectra for chitin and S-chitin demonstrate the interaction and functional groups (Fig. 2b). The sharp and broad absorption peaks from 3400 to 3200 cm−1 correspond to the collective absorption by O–H and N–H groups in the chitin and S-chitin. The secondary amine (N–H) stretch can be indicated by the peaks at 3263 cm−1 in chitin and 3254 cm−1 in S-chitin (Riahi et al., 2023). The NH–CO group in the chitin and S-chitin structure causes absorption peaks at 3114 and 3103 cm−1, respectively (Wijesena et al., 2015). The methyl and methylene groups' stretching mode (C–H bond of sp3-hybridized carbon) attributed to the weak absorption peaks around 2882 to 2951 cm−1. The stretching vibration of CO of the amide I bonds can be ascribed to the absorption peaks around 1658-1614 cm−1. Owing to the impact of hydrogen bonding within the polymeric backbones, the peak has divided into two distinctive bands at 1658 and 1620 cm−1 for chitin, whereas at 1651 and 1624 cm−1 for S-chitin. Absorption peaks around 1430-700 cm−1 could be attributed to the pyranose ring's chemical functionalities, including -C-O-C-, CHx, and –OH (Wijesena et al., 2015). In S-chitin, the additional absorption peak at 654 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching-bending mode of S–O, while the peak located around 528 cm−1 represents the S–S linkage, which confirms the successful functionalization of S-chitin using elemental sulfur imparted to S-chitin by EDA (Priyadarshi et al., 2022, Priyadarshi et al., 2023b). However, the slight shift and decrease in the characteristic peak of the S–S linkage (528 cm−1) may be due to the overlapping functions of the chitin pyranose ring, which appears in a similar IR region (525 cm−1).

XRD diffractograms of chitin and S-chitin composites are depicted in Fig. 2c. For both composites, typical crystal patterns of chitin were observed, attributing to the four characteristics of diffraction peaks at 9.2°, 19.4°, 20.8°, and 23.2° matching to the 0 2 0, 1 1 0, 1 2 0, 1 3 0 crystal planes, respectively (Wijesena et al., 2015). After functionalizing with elemental sulfur, additional peaks of S-chitin in the range of 20°–40° showed the same XRD diffraction peaks as pristine elemental sulfur (Wang et al., 2014). The XRD diffractograms closely overlapped for both composites, confirming that the crystalline structure was maintained during sulfur loading into chitin.

The XPS survey spectra indicated the occurrence of S elements in addition to C, O, and N in the S-chitin complex, corresponding to binding energies around 162.6, 285.4, 298.4, and 531.8 eV (Fig. 2d). As a result of XPS analysis, the atomic concentration of sulfur (S 2p) in the S-chitin complex was measured to be 1.2%, and the atomic concentrations of carbon (C1s), oxygen (O 1s), and nitrogen (N 1s) were measured to be 57.9, 32.7, and 8.2%, respectively (Table S2, Supporting Information). Fig. 2e shows the deconvoluted peak position of C 1s at ∼284.2 (corresponding to C–C/C–H), ∼285.4 (indicating CC/C–N), ∼286.4 eV due to C–O bonding, whereas the peak at 287.3 corresponds to carbonyl group's CO bond (Ehrat et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2015). The N1s spectrum was decomposed into three peaks around 397.8, 398.6, and 399.9 eV, attributed to C–N–C, N-(C)3 (tertiary N bonded to carbon), and N–H groups, respectively (Fig. 2f) (Xia et al., 2004; Zhang et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2015). Fig. 2g displays the three decomposed peaks for the O 1s spectrum with the binding energies at 530.7, 532.0, and 533.1 eV attributed to C–OH/C–O–C, CO, and O–C–O bonds. Further, Fig. 2h indicates that the S 2p3/2 peaks positioned around 160–164 eV (low binding energy region) are attributed to elemental sulfur (Fu et al., 2013). The S 2p spectrum with the peak centered at 161.6 and 162.9 eV confirmed the partly reduced elemental sulfur (S2-8) in the S-chitin composite (Wu et al., 2017). The XPS analysis validates the elemental sulfur involvement in the functionalization of S-chitin by revealing its distinctive structure.

The FE-SEM (Field emission scanning electron microscopy) and EDS (energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy) characterizations were performed to evaluate the surface modification of the chitin and S-chitin composites, and results are shown in Fig. 3 a and b. For the neat chitin, FE-SEM examination showed unevenly structured sheet flakes with different particle distribution morphologies, similar to the reported chitin morphology (Kamal et al., 2020). The microscopic evaluation revealed the morphological changes observed during the S-chitin transformation, indicating that elemental sulfur was effectively synthesized with the chitin matrix. Fig. 3a shows that S-chitin also has a rough surface, but its morphology is more homogeneous than chitin due to the active free sites of elemental sulfur imparted by EDA that keep the structure compact in S-chitin. The rough and unchanging surface is also caused by the small network-like accumulations of elemental sulfur bound together in spherical shapes with chitin in the S-chitin complex.

Additionally, the successful deposition of elemental sulfur on the S-chitin surface was determined by recording EDS spectra and elemental mapping images, as shown in Fig. 3b and c. The elemental sulfur peak at an atomic percentage of 3.5% in the EDS spectrum confirms the presence of elemental sulfur in the S-chitin complex, while the mapping images confirm the homogeneous distribution of sulfur throughout the surface. Other elemental peaks, such as carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, appear at high atomic ratios due to the chitin polymer.

The antioxidant efficacy of S-chitin polymers functionalized with elemental sulfur was assessed using ABTS•+ and DPPH• scavenging strategies and compared to the neat chitin. As shown in Fig. 3d, the neat chitin exhibits negligible radical scavenging efficacy up to a concentration of 200 μg/mL compared to measured S-chitin. In contrast, the antioxidant activity of the S-chitin polymer increased as the concentration increased. For S-chitin, ABTS•+ and DPPH• scavenging efficacies increased to 100% as the concentration rose from 25 to 100 μg/mL. The enhancement of radical scavenging activity in the functionalized polymer (S-chitin) occurred due to the deposition of elemental sulfur using EDA, which increases the availability of electron-rich –NH2 groups and stimulates the hydroxyl groups on the surface of S-chitin polymer (Juarez-Moreno et al., 2015). Regarding the radical scavenging action, S-chitin most probably comprises the physical intercalation or adsorption of radicals where transferring H• from the hydroxyl and amide groups to the DPPH• and ABTS•+ leads to the quenching of free radicals to produce DPPH-H and ABTS-H forms (Priyadarshi et al., 2022).

The antibacterial activity of S-chitin was evaluated against Gram-positive (S. aureus and L. monocytogeneses) and Gram-negative (E. coli and S. enterica) using the agar well diffusion assay, and their MIC/MBC values are listed in Table S3 (Supporting Information). The agar well diffusion analysis shows that the neat chitin did not show growth inhibition for any of the four bacterial strains (Fig. 3e). However, the S-chitin unveiled a distinctive antibacterial effect against S. aureus and L. monocytogenes (Gram-positive) bacterial strains, but less antibacterial effect against E. coli and S. enterica (Gram-negative). In addition, the S-chitin showed growth inhibition zones of ∼6.6 mm, ∼4.7 mm, and ∼2.1 mm, ∼1.5 mm inhibition zone against L. monocytogenes and S. aureus and then S. enterica and E. coli, respectively. However, no inhibition zone was observed for the neat chitin against all four bacterial strains (Table S3). Likewise, the MIC/MBC evaluation depicted that the antibacterial efficacy of S-chitin was similar to that of the agar well diffusion assay. The MIC/MBC of S-chitin against L. monocytogenes and S. aureus were 200/300 and 300/400 μg/mL, while S. enterica and E. coli were 600/700 and 800/900 μg/mL, respectively. On the other side, the neat chitin had MIC/MBC >1000 μg/mL against all four bacterial strains, suggesting a negligible bactericidal effect (Table S3). Therefore, it can be validated that the antibacterial effect of S-chitin is ascribed to the functionalization of chitin by the sulfur. These antibacterial properties of S-chitin are reliable with the results of Dop et al., who recently investigated that a high-sulfur polymer developed using reverse vulcanization showed strong antibacterial activity against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria (Dop et al., 2023), and they suggested that the interaction of polymer particles with cellular thiols could be a possible mechanism of action against bacterial cells. Sulfur in various forms (e.g., sulfur nanoparticles, sulfur polymers, or sulfur quantum dots) has been reported to exhibit more substantial bactericidal impacts against Gram-positive bacterial strains than Gram-negative bacterial strains (Dop et al., 2023; Priyadarshi et al., 2023a; Riahi et al., 2022; Saedi et al., 2020). Several possible mechanisms have been projected regarding the antimicrobial efficacy of different forms of sulfur particles. For instance, interactions with biological molecules on the surface of bacteria can cause cell lysis due to membrane rupturing and produce toxic H2S gas when lipids and proteins interact with their thiol groups and interact with DNA to cause cell death by denaturing DNA (Dop et al., 2023; Priyadarshi et al., 2023a; Riahi et al., 2022; Saedi et al., 2020). Also, the micro-sized elemental sulfur's antibacterial action was caused by its binding to an enzyme with a –SH functional group (Saedi et al., 2020). However, the antimicrobial results depicted that the antibacterial function of S-chitin was more potent in Gram-positive bacteria (single thick peptidoglycan layer) than in Gram-negative bacteria (thin peptidoglycan layer with an outer cell wall), which can correspond to the variation in their cell wall structure (Khan et al., 2023b).

FTIR analysis reveals the fabricated films' functional groups and the interactions between the polymer matrix and the added fillers. The FTIR spectra of the neat CS/Gel, CS/Gel@S-chitin2, and CS/Gel@S-chitin4 films (Fig. 4 a) show the peaks ascribed to CS, Gel, and S-chitin composite, like stretching bending in the –OH/-NH, and CO groups around ∼3286 and ∼1644 cm−1, whereas vibration stretching in N–H of amide II around 1556 cm−1 (Amide-II) (Khan et al., 2024; Minhas et al., 2023). The absorption peak at 1576 cm−1 could be attributed to the overlapping of –COO- asymmetric stretching and deformation vibration of –NH2. The fundamental absorption around 1023, 1053, and 14011 cm−1 are due to the C–N, C–O, and –COO– stretching vibrations, respectively. The peaks corresponding to Gel were noticed at 1110 cm−1, indicating the stretching bending in the –COOH group (Khan et al., 2024; Minhas et al., 2023). The FTIR spectra for all the films show no significant difference in the fundamental peaks, which represent CS, Gel, and S-chitin due to similar arrangements were superimposed on each other as peaks were overlapped at 1453 cm−1 (symmetrical stretching of the nitro group), 1539 cm−1 (C–H bonds), and 1644 cm−1 (CO) in the CS/Gel@S-chitin films (Aqib et al., 2021). But after compositing S-chitin, the characteristic absorption peak for composited films was observed to slightly downshift from 3286, 1556 cm−1 (CS/Gel) to 3277, 1539 cm−1 (CS/Gel@S-chitin2, and CS/Gel@S-chitin4) owing to the intermolecular hydrogen bonds between added fillers and polymer matrices. However, a new absorption peak around 653 cm−1 in CS/Gel@S-chitin2 and CS/Gel@S-chitin4 is attributed to the stretching bending mode of the S–O linkage, which confirms the successful incorporation of S-chitin and crosslinking with CS/Gel chain.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the CS/Gel, CS/Gel@S-chitin2, and CS/Gel@S-chitin4 films are depicted in Fig. 4b. The broad peak at 20.7° for the CS/Gel, CS/Gel@S-chitin2, and CS/Gel@S-chitin4 films is due to its anhydrous crystalline states, indicating the amorphous nature of the biopolymer films (Ali et al., 2023). In addition, the broadening of peak (∼20°) of the CS/Gel@S-chitin2 and CS/Gel@S-chitin4 films in comparison to the neat CS/Gel film may be attributed to the enhanced interaction between S-chitin and CS/Gel matrices where the impregnation of functional filler wrecks the anhydrous crystalline structure of composite films. Moreover, additional diffraction peaks in the CS/Gel@S-chitin2 and CS/Gel@S-chitin4 films are similar to the S-chitin (Fig. 2c), indicating the presence of S-chitin within the composite film without changes in the crystal structure.

The XPS survey and core level spectra were conducted to investigate the different elemental compositions of the neat CS/Gel and CS/Gel@S-chitin4 films, as depicted in Fig. 4c–g. The binding energy values represent the valence state and charge density around the atoms. XPS survey (Fig. 4c) showed the elemental composition of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s peaks on the surface of the CS/Gel and CS/Gel@S-chitin4 films corresponded to the binding energies around 282.6, 298.9, and 531.9 eV, respectively. However, the supplementary S 2p peak at ∼166.2 eV corresponds to the effective impregnation of the S-chitin in the CS/Gel@S-chitin4 film (Fig. 4c). The deconvoluted C 1s spectrum of the neat-CS/Gel film can be split into four peaks at binding energies of 283.4, 284.8, 285.3, and 286.9 eV, corresponding to the C–C/C–H, CC/C–N, C–O, and CO or O–C–O (carbonyl) species, respectively (Fig. 4d). Similar to the neat-CS/Gel film, the deconvoluted C 1 s spectrum of the CS/Gel@S-chitin4 film can be split into four peaks at binding energies of 283.7 eV (C–C/C–H), 285.1 eV (C–N/CC), 286.6 eV (C–O), and 287.1 eV (CO or O–C–O) (Fig. 4d), corresponding to hydroxyl, amino, epoxy, and carboxyl groups, respectively. The same peaks for the deconvoluted C 1s spectra for both films with slight shifting at binding energies concluded that the hydrogen-bonding interaction impregnated the S-chitin in the CS/Gel matrices (Li et al., 2023). For deconvoluted O 1s form CO/N–CO, C–O–C/C–OH, and O–C–O groups can be designated by the characteristics peaks positioned at ∼530.2, 531.4, and 532.2 eV, respectively (Fig. 4e) (Zhang et al., 2015). The N 1s spectra of both films (Fig. 4f) exhibited four peaks at binding energies of 398.5 and 399.2 eV, indicating the C–N–C and –NH2 group, whereas N–H/C–N and NH3 + species can be attributed to the binding energies at 399.4 and 400.6 eV, respectively (Khan et al., 2023c). In addition, Fig. 4g shows two peaks at 161.9 and 162.5 eV corresponding to the partly reduced compound elemental sulfur (S2-8) in S-chitin (Wu et al., 2017), whereas the binding energies around ∼ 167.3 eV, ∼168.4 eV correspond to the sulfonate (-OSO3 2− (S 2p3/2), and -OSO3 2− (S 2p1/2) groups (Khan et al., 2023d, e).

Surface and cross-section microstructures of the neat-CS/Gel, CS/Gel@S-chitin2 and CS/Gel@S-chitin4 films were observed via FE-SEM (Fig. 5 a). A continuous smooth surface without irregularities, voids, or pores was observed in the neat-CS/Gel film. On the other hand, adding S-chitin had a minute effect on the surface structure of CS/Gel@S-chitin2 film, and in CS/Gel@S-chitin4 film, the filler particles were evenly distributed at different locations. The film surface shows wavy lumps and fine curves because of the addition of solids. However, the compactness and homogeneity of the composite film (CS/Gel@S-chitin4) remain intact even after adding 4 wt% of S-chitin. Additionally, the cross-sectional view of the fabricated film depicts an intact microstructure without pores, cavities, or cleaves, suggesting an excellent affinity with S-chitin and CS/Gel composites.

Moreover, the elemental mapping images and EDS spectra shown in Figs. S1a–d exhibited the peaks of C, N and O on the surface of the neat CS/Gel and CS/Gel@S-chitin4 films corresponding to the major constitutional elements in fabricating the composite structure. However, an additional sulfur peak for CS/Gel@S-chitin4 film validated the existence of S-chitin in the composite film. It is worth noting that elemental mapping images showed that these elements were evenly dispersed on the film surface, further confirming the homogeneous mixing in the film matrix.

Incorporating S-chitin into the CS/Gel film significantly changed the visual appearance of the composite films (Fig. 5b). Accordingly, the film's apparent color (the total color difference (ΔE) and L*, a*, and b*-values) changed significantly, as listed in Table 1 . The lightness (L*) for the neat-CS/Gel film was 89.4, corresponding to the transparency of the film, and it decreased to 81.7 when 4 wt% of S-chitin was incorporated. The color of the composite film appeared yellow-brown, and transparency slightly decreased. Due to the yellow color of S-chitin, the b*-value (yellowness) of the film enhanced significantly from 12.1 (neat-CS/Gel film) to 38.9 (CS/Gel@S-chitin4 film). Additionally, the loading of S-chitin increased the redness (a*-value) of the composite films from 1.7 to 4.9. Consequently, with the addition of S-chitin, the ΔE value also significantly increased from 8.4 (neat-CS/Gel film) to 36.4 (CS/Gel@S-chitin4 film).

Additionally, the effect of S-chitin addition on the UV-blocking and transparency characteristics of the composite films was evaluated by recording UV–vis spectra in the range of 200–800 nm, and the results are depicted in Fig. 5c and d and Table 1.

It can be clearly observed from UV–vis light transmittance spectra (Fig. 5c, Table 1) that the neat-CS/Gel film was able to transmit 90.5% of irradiated light in the visible region (T660). This investigation confirms that the neat CS/Gel film was substantially transparent. However, the transparency of the film decreased significantly (p < 0.05) with the addition of S-chitin. As the S-chitin concentration increased, the films' light transmittance at 660 nm (T660) decreased from 90.5% to 63.4%. On the other hand, the UV-blocking ability of the film determined by the light transmittance at 280 nm (T280) decreased dramatically from 13.4 to 0.05% (Table 1). There has been active discussion about the decrease in visible light transmittance due to the addition of solids (Khan et al., 2023b). The UV region also exhibited a small peak near ∼280 nm, indicating the π-π* transition of the biopolymer's carboxylic group (CO). Furthermore, with the addition of 4 wt% S-chitin, the composite film was able to block 98.7% of UV-A and 100% of UV-B light (Fig. 5d). These results mean that adding S-chitin to CS/Gel film can provide excellent UV protection, ensure photo-oxidation safety of high-fat foods, and extend shelf life (Uthirakumar et al., 2018).

The mechanical characteristics of the films, including thickness, elongation at break, tensile strength, and elastic modulus, are shown in Table 2 . The thickness of the neat-CS/Gel film significantly increased (p < 0.05) from 72.8 to 119.4 μm by the addition of the S-chitin fillers. The neat-CS/Gel film was relatively strong and brittle (TS = 46.7 MPa; EB = 4.5%, and EM = 1.9 GPa). The addition of S-chitin affected the mechanical properties of the CS/Gel film. In particular, the strength (TS) of the film was significantly (p < 0.05) increased by the addition of S-chitin and increased by 7.9% and 18.4% by the addition of 2% and 4% of S-chitin. The increase in TS of CS/Gel@S-chitin films can be due to the increase of intermolecular attraction between S-chitin and CS/Gel matrix, which means that S-chitin interacted compatible with CS/Gel matrix. Previous studies have demonstrated that incorporating functional fillers into biopolymer blends can increase mechanical strength (Roy & Rhim, 2020).

The WCA is an imperative property for assessing the surface wettability of packaging films. The WCA of the CS/Gel film was 62.9o, which can be considered to have a hydrophilic surface like other hydrophilic biopolymer blended films (Table 2) (Khan et al., 2023b; Khan et al., 2023d). The WCA of the film increased significantly (p < 0.05) with the incorporation of S-chitin. The increased WCA of the composite films can be due to the more hydrophobic nature of S-chitin than the CS/Gel matrix. Additionally, the increase in WCA of composite films could be caused by electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonds between amino and hydroxyl groups of CS/Gel and S-chitin. These chemical interactions can reduce the exposure of hydrophilic groups and induce molecular reorientation on the film surface, thereby increasing WCA.

WVP is also a crucial property in food packaging applications because low WVP can slow the water vapor transfer rate between the environment and the food (Flórez et al., 2022). As depicted in Table 2, the WVP of the neat CS/Gel film was 9.7 × 10−10 g m/m2.Pa.s, constant with the previously published result (Khan et al., 2023b). The WVP of the film decreased significantly (p < 0.05) with the addition of S-chitin, which may be because S-chitin particles are more hydrophobic than the CS/Gel matrix. S-chitin can create a discontinuous phase in the polymer matrix, altering the free volume of the polymer interface and reducing the amount of polar water vapor molecules that can pass through (Roy et al., 2019; Yadav et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant and antimicrobial activities of the films
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of S-chitin on the antioxidant capacity of CS/Gel films was investigated by evaluating the free radical scavenging potential of ABTS•+ and DPPH•, as presented in Fig. 6 a. The antioxidant activity of the films increased significantly (p < 0.05) with increasing S-chitin content, which was attributed to the surface hydroxyl and electron-rich –NH2 groups of chitin being functionalized with sulfur endowed by ethylene diamine (Juarez-Moreno et al., 2015). Transferring H• from the hydroxyl and amide groups to the DPPH• and ABTS•+ quenches free radicals into DPPH-H and ABTS-H (Priyadarshi et al., 2022). The obtained scavenging results suggest that the antioxidant efficacy of CS/Gel-based films is significantly enhanced after enriching with S-chitin, which may be useful for industrial applications, including oxidation-sensitive food packaging.

The antimicrobial potential of the CS/Gel-based films against E. coli and L. monocytogenes is depicted in Fig. 6b and c. Interestingly, the neat CS/Gel film displayed a significant antibacterial effect, showing a bacteriostatic or slight bactericidal effect due to chitosan's antibacterial activity (Priyadarshi & Rhim, 2020). On the other hand, S-chitin-containing films showed strong antibacterial activity directly dependent on the concentration of added filler. In particular, films with 4 wt% S-chitin (CS/Gel@S-chitin4) exhibited strong bactericidal activity against the test foodborne pathogens, which completely stopped the growth of L. monocytogenes and E. coli after 3 h and 12 h incubation, respectively. The antibacterial test results of the composite films also showed that the CS/Gel@S-chitin films displayed more pronounced antibacterial potential towards Gram-positive bacteria (L. monocytogenes) than Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli), which is consistent with the antibacterial test results of S-chitin (Fig. 3e, Table S3). E. coli strains are less susceptible to sulfur-functionalized S-chitin complexes because the outer cells (peptidoglycan cell wall surrounded by an outer membrane containing lipopolysaccharides) protect Gram-negative strains (Saedi et al., 2020). In contrast, the cell wall of Gram-positive bacteria with a thicker peptidoglycan layer without an outer membrane makes it more susceptible to S-chitin interaction with the cell interior (Saedi et al., 2020). The high antibacterial activity of CS/Gel@S-chitin film may be the synergistic reaction of chitosan and S-chitin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CS/Gel@S-chitin films for chicken preservation
ce:section.ce:para:
Fresh chicken is susceptible to microbial spoilage and oxidation (oxidation of pigments, proteins, and lipids) even at low temperatures during storage and transportation (Yang et al., 2023). Therefore, packaging materials with bioactive capabilities are needed to preserve processed meat during storage and throughout the supply chain. While the chicken was packaged with neat-CS/Gel-based film and stored at 4 °C for 20 days, changes in the appearance, chroma value (
a
* 2
+
b
* 2
), pH, TBARS, and TVC were measured, and the results are shown in Fig. 7 . As shown in Fig. 7a, the chicken of all packaging samples showed changes in appearance as the storage period increased. Depending on the type of packaging film, the longer the storage period, the darker the surface of the chicken became. The surface color of chicken packaged with the neat CS/Gel film began to change after 5 days of storage, while chicken packaged with films containing S-chitin showed insignificant color change.

Discoloration of meat products indicates a decline in meat quality, a key characteristic that determines consumer acceptance of food (Khan et al., 2023a). The surface color quality of the meat is usually assessed using a colorimeter. As depicted in Fig. 7b, the chroma value of the chicken packaged with the neat CS/Gel film changed more rapidly than that packaged with the CS/Gel@S-chitin films. Typically, meat changes color in fresh packaged meat due to microbial growth during storage, which lowers the oxygen content of the surface tissue and causes the myoglobin to oxidize to produce metmyoglobin, which turns the meat brown (Katiyo et al., 2020). Therefore, the increase in the chroma value of chicken during storage is usually associated with the gradual formation of metmyoglobin, which causes meat discoloration (Gurunathan et al., 2022). These findings indicate that the CS/Gel@S-chitin-based films effectively preserve the aesthetic quality of packaged meat. However, the myoglobin content in chicken is known to be low. Therefore, this phenomenon is more evident in red meat than in chicken muscle (Katiyo et al., 2020). Previous investigations have suggested that raw chicken's microbial spoilage, pH, and TBARS signals may be more reliable parameters than appearance (Cai et al., 2021; Gurunathan et al., 2022; Katiyo et al., 2020).

Additionally, pH and lipid oxidation values are important in evaluating meat quality (Gurunathan et al., 2022). Changes in pH and TBARS values of chicken meat during storage are shown in Fig. 7c and d. The initial pH of fresh chicken was around 5.9 ± 0.06, and the pH values of all groups increased significantly over time and reached the maximum packaged with the neat CS/Gel film (pH 6.8 ± 0.04) (Fig. 7c). The slight increase in the pH of chicken in the early stage of storage may be due to the oxidation of chicken lipids and the glycolysis of glycogen. As storage time passes, the production of nitrogen-containing compounds due to the decomposition of amino acids and proteins increases alkalinity, increasing pH (Cai et al., 2021). The pH value of chicken packaged with the CS/Gel@S-chitin films showed less change than the control group. In particular, chicken packaged with the CS/Gel@S-chitin4 film has a low pH reduction rate and maintains good quality with a pH of 6.18 ± 0.4 (below 6.2 for the acceptance limit) even after 20 days of storage. Generally, if the pH increases from 6.2 to 6.5, chicken is considered spoiled and unfit for consumption (Kim et al., 2022).

Unsaturated fatty acids are more likely to be oxidized during storage, and the degree of oxidation of fatty acids in meat is generally determined using the TBARS value (Lin, Mei, et al., 2023). Changes in TBARS values of the packaged chicken during storage are displayed in Fig. 7d. The initial TBARS value for the fresh chicken was 0.14 ± 0.08 mg MDA/kg, which increased steadily to 1.7 ± 0.05 mg MDA/kg at 20 days of storage when packaged with the neat CS/Gel film. However, the TBARS values of the chicken packaged with CS/Gel@S-chitin films increased more slowly. The TBARS value of chicken packaged with CS/Gel@S-chitin2 (1.1 ± 0.03 mg MDA/kg) and CS/Gel@S-chitin4 (0.9 ± 0.05 mg MDA/kg) composite films reduced the TBARS values by 35.3% and 46.5%, respectively, to the control group, which is primarily because of the antioxidant action of the S-chitin (Fig. 3d). The TBARS value of chicken packaged with CS/Gel@S-chitin4 and stored for 20 days did not surpass the standard value (1 mg MDA/kg), which is within the range suitable for consumption (Hassan et al., 2022). The results indicate that the S-chitin-containing composite film can delay the oxidation of chicken meat, maintaining meat quality and extending its shelf life.

The total viable colony count (TVC) is another important quality factor for ensuring meat quality (Khan et al., 2023c). As depicted in Fig. 7e, the initial value of the TVC in the fresh chicken was about 1.9 ± 0.1 log CFU/g. The TVC value of chicken packaged with the neat CS/Gel film increased significantly over time, exceeding 7.6 ± 0.2 log CFU/g at day 20, whereas that of chicken packaged with CS/Gel@S-chitin2 and CS/Gel@S-chitin4 films increased to 6.0 ± 0.2 and 5.4 ± 0.1 log CFU/g, respectively, on day 20. The TVC value of chicken packaged with CS/Gel@S-chitin4 composite film was lower than that of the control group (by 2.2 log CFU/g) and within the maximum permissible limits of chicken consumption (7 log CFU/g) (Mexis et al., 2012). As a result, it can be seen that the CS/Gel@S-chitin films could effectively inhibit the growth of microorganisms in chickens, ensure safety, and extend shelf life.

The chicken packaging study showed that the CS/Gel@S-chitin film effectively preserved chicken stored at 4 °C due to the synergistic impact of the film's antioxidant, antibacterial and UV-blocking properties.

Sulfur-chitin composite (S-chitin) was prepared using ethylenediamine by forming a sulfur-amine complex. The S-chitin showed potent antibacterial, antioxidant, and UV-barrier properties. The S-chitin was used to fabricate active packaging film by blending with chitosan/gelatin (CS/Gel) polymer blend. The S-chitin-added composite films (CS/Gel@S-chitin) showed increased mechanical, water vapor barrier, and functional properties. The CS/Gel@S-chitin films were used for chicken packaging and showed high potential for maintaining quality, securing safety, and extending the shelf life of chicken. In conclusion, the sulfur-amine composite functionalized with sulfur used in this study can fabricate biopolymer packaging films with various functions to meet consumer demands for environmentally friendly active packaging to replace non-degradable plastics.

Ajahar Khan: Conceptualization, methodology, investigation, data curation, visualization, writing-original draft. Zohreh Riahi: Methodology, investigation, writing -original draft, visualization, data curation. Jun Tae Kim: Writing-review & editing, Data curation. Jong-Whan Rhim: Supervision, writing-review & editing, funding acquisition, project administration.
